query: MongoDB write throughput scalability 50000 writes per second sharding performance 2024
answer: MongoDB scales write throughput by sharding, distributing data across multiple nodes to handle 50,000 writes per second efficiently. Shard key selection and horizontal scaling are crucial for performance. Sharding allows linear scalability beyond single-node limits.

results.title: Scaling MongoDB to 100K+ Writes per Second
results.snippet: DATA, AI, AND THE REAL WORLD. # Scaling MongoDB to 100K+ Writes per Second. Sustaining 100K+ writes per second in MongoDB is not a tuning trick — it is an architectural decision. This article breaks down how to design a sharded cluster using realistic Atlas hardware (32GB RAM, 8 CPU, standard storage) and achieve linear horizontal scaling through deterministic shard key distribution, clean write paths, and disciplined index strategy. > Scaling MongoDB to 100K Writes per Second is not a tuning exercise — it is an architectural decision. This article breaks down how to design a sharded cluster using realistic Atlas hardware (32GB RAM, 8 CPU, standard storage) and achieve linear horizontal scaling through deterministic shard key distribution, clean write…. ## Why Scaling MongoDB to 100K Writes per Second Requires Horizontal Sharding ? Scaling MongoDB to 100K writes per second cannot be achieved with vertical upgrades alone because a single node—even with generous RAM, CPU, and I/O—quickly reaches physical limits.

results.title: How MongoDB Handles High Traffic and Large Datasets - DEV Community
results.snippet: Its distributed architecture combines replica sets, sharding, and intelligent query routing to handle high traffic and large datasets without requiring you to endlessly upgrade a single server. **Config server**s: A replica set that stores the cluster’s metadata — specifically, the mapping of which chunk ranges live on which shard. ├──► Shard 1 (replica set: primary + 2 secondaries). ├──► Shard 2 (replica set: primary + 2 secondaries). └──► Shard 3 (replica set: primary + 2 secondaries). The shard key determines how data is distributed across shards, which queries can be routed efficiently, and whether write traffic is balanced or concentrated on a single shard. When you shard a collection, you specify a **shard key** — a field or combination of fields that MongoDB uses to divide data into chunks and assign those chunks to shards. `// Range shard key — efficient range queries, risk of write hotspots on monotonic fields. // Hashed shard key — even write distribution, no range query efficiency.
